coredata.dc:title: A simplified 3D-resolved microstructure model for high-fidelity lithium-ion battery cell simulations
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Alberghini, Matteo
Blanco, Giulia
Bertinetti, Andrea
Tommasi, Alessio
Sgroi, Mauro
coredata.dc:description: Reliable and representative 3D microstructures of lithium-ion cells are essential for identifying novel and cost-effective optimization strategies for next-generation battery technologies. Numerical simulations are a powerful tool to generate microstructures and evaluate their impact on the cell performance without relying on expensive imaging techniques. This study introduces a simplified porous cathode model using 3D-resolved geometric entities to represent each phase. Specifically, the impact of three levels of detail in modeling additive conductive materials, such as carbon black, is investigated: via (i) sub-micron additive particles, (ii) micron-sized aggregates, or (iii) homogenized within the catholyte, as commonly assumed. Electrode geometries are generated and analyzed via a dedicated script implemented in Octave. Equivalent cathodic properties and cell performance are evaluated via finite elements simulations to assess the impact of the three modeling methods considered. Results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach with respect to homogenized structures, successfully matching experimental benchmarks of electrode conductivity and tortuosity, and avoiding overestimation of cell performance. These findings pave the wave to ease the simulation of accurate cell microstructures, improving the reliability of 3D simulations and fostering more computationally-efficient models.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion batteries
Microscale modeling
Electrochemical modeling
3D-resolved microstructure
Finite Elements Method

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was performed as part of the SOLiDIFY project, funded by the European Union's Horizon 2020 research and innovation program under grant agreement No. 875557, and the HEDAsupercap project, funded by the European Union's Horizon Europe research and innovation action under grant agreement No. 101092189.

This research was performed within the MODALIS 2 and SOLiDIFY projects, respectively funded by the European Union\u2019s Horizon 2020 research and innovation program under grant agreements No. 875193 and No. 875557 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para:
Setup schematics: (A) Schematic of the simulated solid-state battery cell, highlighting the materials included and the thickness of each component. (B–C) Configurations generated considering small (B) and large (C) box sizes, namely
B
x
= 20
m and
B
x
= 30
μm, respectively. (D) Comparison between numerical (red, green and blue data) and experimental (black data) particle size distributions (PSD). (E-F) Simplification of the geometry obtained, shown in panel B, by replacing the CB clusters contributing to the electric percolation of with equivalent cylinders or spheres. The unclustered CB particles connecting two NMC spheres in Panel E were shown in red. CB particles or clusters that do not connect two or more NMC particles were removed. Panel F highlights the geometric features of the two equivalent cluster representations considered (see Section 3). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Microstructural properties as a function of CB volume fraction.: (A) Percentage of NMC particles isolated from the current collector. (B) Percentage of CB particles not contributing to the connectivity of the active material. (C) Average neighbors number per NMC particle. (D) Percentage of NMC surface available for Li intercalation (namely, in contact withe the SC). (E) Average number of CB particles per cluster. (F) Average cluster electrical conductivity evaluated via fractal aggregation theory (see Methods). All of the mean values shown (black diamonds) were obtained by averaging 30 configurations, 10 for each box size considered.

Equivalent cathode properties: (A) Comparison of the effective cathode electrical conductivity between the considered microstructure models. Cylindrical and spherical clusters are represented in green; black and purple spheres respectively represent CB and NMC. Dashed lines were only reported for eye guidance. Experimental data from Refs. [21] (solid black line) and [22] (dashed black line). (B) Comparison of the solid catholyte tortuosity between the considered microstructure models. Experimental data from Refs. [23] (solid black line) and [24] (dashed black line). Analytical data for tortuosity (solid gray line) were evaluated via the Bruggeman model for spherical obstacles [25]. Considering each value of
ϕ
C B
, the data shown were evaluated by averaging the values obtained for three different microstructures. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Comparison between the electrochemical performance of the tested microstructure models: (A) Cell voltage as a function of the state of charge for
ϕ
C B
= 4 . 8 %
. (B) 3D maps of the relative lithium concentration in the configuration investigated. The images were generated at the end of the charging transient for
ϕ
C B
= 4 . 8 %
. The color bar refers to all the three figures; red spheres in Conf.2 and Conf. 3 are NMC particles not connected by the CB network to the positive current collector. The abbreviation CC+ stands for: positive current collector. (C) Cell voltage as a function of the state of charge for
ϕ
C B
= 7 . 5 %
(red curves) and
ϕ
C B
= 10 %
(black curves). Curves for both values of
ϕ
C B
evaluated with Conf. 1 overlap. (D–E) Comparison between the percentage of NMC particles isolated from the positive current collector (panel D) and average neighbor number per NMC particle (panel E) considering either explicit (cyan data) or spherically-clustered (magenta data) models for the CB particles. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Comparison between the energy stored and released with different applied currents, considering homogenized (Conf.1) or explicit (Conf. 2) model for the carbon additive. The simulations were performed considering
ϕ
C B
= 10 %
. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Effect of microstructure variability on cell performance: (A) Representation of a NMC particle (red sphere) with 11 neighbors (blue spheres). CB particles are represented with black spheres. (B–C) Cell potential as a function of the SOC (panel B) and energy transferred (panel C) for three different configurations obtained with
B
x
= 20
μm and one with
B
x
= 30
μm. (D) Normalized lithium concentration at the end of charging for the configurations tested. Reps. 1–3 refer to replicas generated with the same microstructure parameters (i.e., AM and SC volume fractions, and box size) and resulting in different NMC and CB arrangements. Rep. 1 refers to the same configuration shown in Fig. 4. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: A simplified 3D-resolved microstructure model for high-fidelity lithium-ion battery cell simulations

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Gemmate Technologies, via Reano 31, Buttigliera Alta, 10090, Italy
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Gemmate Technologies, via Reano 31, Buttigliera Alta, 10090, Italia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Turin – Department of Chemistry and NIS, Via Pietro Giuria 7, Turin, 10125, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Turin – Department of Chemistry and NIS

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: University of Turin – Department of Chemistry and NIS, Via Pietro Giuria 7, Turin, 10125, Italia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reliable and representative 3D microstructures of lithium-ion cells are essential for identifying novel and cost-effective optimization strategies for next-generation battery technologies. Numerical simulations are a powerful tool to generate microstructures and evaluate their impact on the cell performance without relying on expensive imaging techniques. This study introduces a simplified porous cathode model using 3D-resolved geometric entities to represent each phase. Specifically, the impact of three levels of detail in modeling additive conductive materials, such as carbon black, is investigated: via (i) sub-micron additive particles, (ii) micron-sized aggregates, or (iii) homogenized within the catholyte, as commonly assumed. Electrode geometries are generated and analyzed via a dedicated script implemented in Octave. Equivalent cathodic properties and cell performance are evaluated via finite elements simulations to assess the impact of the three modeling methods considered. Results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach with respect to homogenized structures, successfully matching experimental benchmarks of electrode conductivity and tortuosity, and avoiding overestimation of cell performance. These findings pave the wave to ease the simulation of accurate cell microstructures, improving the reliability of 3D simulations and fostering more computationally-efficient models.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Part of the data was made available as Supplementary Material. Remaining data will be available on reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para:
Solid-state batteries have emerged as a viable alternative to traditional liquid-based lithium-ion batteries, offering improved cost efficiency, safety, and environmental impact. Chlorine-rich lithium argyrodite (Li
6
PS
5
Cl) has proven to be a promising solid-state electrolyte due to its high ionic conductivity, good compatibility with protected lithium metal anodes, and electrochemical stability [1]. The use of a solid state electrolyte requires the creation of a composite cathode microstructure that includes the active cathodic material, conductive agents such as carbon, and a catholyte, which is the fraction of the solid state electrolyte present in the cathode structure. However, the use of such catholytes still poses several challenges, such as understanding and managing complex chemical processes and maintaining structural cell integrity. Numerical simulations are a powerful tool to understand the effects of the coupled physical phenomena that control battery performance and to optimize their design at the microstructure level. However, achieving a balance between accuracy and computational cost, particularly in capturing the complexity of 3D cell structures, remains a significant challenge.

The basis for understanding the complex interplay between electrochemical and mechanical phenomena within a battery is provided by realistic microstructure models. These models allow the study of geometric features, porosity, tortuosity, and interfaces, providing insight into how these factors affect battery performance and aging: the fidelity of microstructure models is critical to achieve comprehensive and predictive battery simulations. Deriving the microstructures from experimental data, such as FIB-SEM images or X-ray tomographies, is the best strategy to obtain realistic setups with the highest fidelity. Several numerical studies applied such techniques to reconstruct portion of the cell electrode and perform electrochemical simulations [2–7]. However, this strategy is experimentally slow and expensive, while the resulting geometry generally requires high computational times to be reconstructed and simulated. Moreover, these techniques can mostly be applied a-posteriori: the analysis can only be performed on components after their manufacture, which lacks the flexibility to explore different microstructural parameters and restricts the exploration of different geometric features. In fact, most of the cited work has focused its analysis on gaining more insight into a single electrode structure than what could be achieved by traditional cyclic voltammetry testing alone; their analysis could be extended to other microstructures only after manufacturing and analyzing new dedicated samples. Conversely, simplified models [8–10] may miss the level of detail required to represent all the main transfer processes with sufficient accuracy. In this sense, the role of percolation of carbon aggregates is often neglected, even though its function is known to be crucial in reducing ohmic losses of batteries [11]. Numerical studies generally assume an overlap between the active material particles and a homogeneous distribution of carbon black (CB) in the catholyte [9,10,12,13]. This leads to an erroneous estimation of battery performance and properties, and does not allow exploiting at its best a 3D electrode description. Only a few seminal studies started to investigate the effects of generating numerically the whole cathode microstructure and/or investigate the effect of dispersing conductive additives on simulated or reconstructed geometries. In particular, Bielefeld et al. [14] used the software GeoDict to create several electrode microstructures and study the effective properties of the generated configurations. However, they do not perform electrochemical simulations of full or half cells, which are fundamental to assess the effect of the different model assumptions. Then, Randau and coworkers [15] numerically tested the effect of different shapes of conductive additives dispersed in the simulation domain. However, to simplify the domain, electrochemical simulations were performed considering an homogenization approach: the conductive additives were dispersed only in a reduced cell element, which was used to evaluate effective properties considered as representative of the whole cell; then, the electrochemical simulations were performed considering an effective electrolyte domain with both ionic and electrical conductivity properties. Finally, Joos et al. [16] proposed an interesting hybrid approach consisting of mapping particles of active material and additives with different shapes by FIB/SEM tomography, storing them in a database, and then using them to build new electrode structures by randomly extracting particles from their library. This approach combines high fidelity data and flexibility, allowing new cathode structures to be built; however, the resulting structures retain an extremely complex geometry that is difficult to manage for a systematic study of the cell topology and performance.

In view of this analysis, there is a noticeable gap in the literature for a simple but reliable cathode microstructure generator capable of capturing the main transport phenomena and, at the same time, estimating the effect of non ideal electrical percolation based only on few input cell parameters.

This work presents a novel design approach that aims to improve the representation of 3D-resolved electrodes while avoiding high computational costs of reconstructing complex geometries. Instead, all electrode components are automatically generated from basic shapes such as spheres, cubes, and cylinders; these geometries match the target volume fractions of each component, the particle size distributions of the active material, and, importantly, aim to capture the effects of the CB networks. The concept is applied to a solid-state argyrodite-based cell, with a lithium metal anode and NMC811 as cathodic active material. Section 2 outlines the simulation setup and the numerical protocol used to construct the cathode geometries, implemented through a custom code developed using the Octave programming language. Subsequently, Section 3 presents the main properties of the generated microstructure, along with a box-size independence study to prove the reliability of the investigated setups, both performed as a function of the volume fraction of conductive carbon additives considered. In Section 4, the proposed methodology is validated by comparing the equivalent cathode electric conductivity and catholyte tortuosity to literature references. Additionally, the possibility of simplifying the CB network by representing only the active CB particles, organized in clusters, is explored. Finally, in Section 5 electrochemical simulations are used to assess the impact of different microstructure models on the overall cell performance.

In conclusion, this work presents a self-consistent and efficient modeling approach for particles-based electrodes, able to recover their main transport properties without relying on external characterizations, 3D reconstructions or images, and software. Following the models classification provided by Bielefeld [17], this work differs from the previous references by aiming to bridge two different scales of battery models: on the one hand conduction networks, which focus on the electronic and/or ionic conduction pathways; on the other hand, simplified but representative electrochemical simulations, avoiding excessive detail while being able to recover representative effective properties. Overall, by balancing accuracy and simplicity, it aims to enable the community to easily explore new electrode design strategies and, in perspective, improve the cell performance.

The simulation setup, shown in Fig. 1A, was built following the experimental parameters and procedures used for manufacturing laboratory-level argyrodite-based coin cells. The cell anode was modeled as an ideal lithium plate; instead, the cathode was modeled as composed by a mixture of NMC811 (purple spheres), commercial carbon-based additive SuperP (CB, black spheres), and argyrodite sulfide (Li6PS5Cl) as a solid catholyte (SC). The SC, as well as the electrolyte used as separator, was modeled as a continuous homogeneous medium (gray domain within the cathode); instead, the NMC particles were supposed to preserve their original particles size distribution (PSD), namely particles with diameters in the range 2 μm – 11 μm. Finally, the CB was modeled with particles with diameter in the range 0.25 μm – 0.75 μm [18].

The electrodes are separated by a thick layer of argyrodite (green domain), while the cathode is connected to an aluminum plate. The details of the cell architecture taken as a reference are summarized in Table 1. However, note that the volume fraction of CB, which nominal value is 4.8%, was varied up to 10% to assess how its effect on the equivalent cathode properties and the overall cell performance. The resulting cell architecture provides a nominal capacity of 1.47 mAh/cm2.

The packing of NMC spheres was obtained by a fall-and-roll approach [8,16]. Particles are randomly generated at the top of the simulation box and allowed to fall vertically until they reach the bottom of the box or hit another particle that has already settled. The code interface allows to set the target volume fraction of active material, particle size distribution, size of the simulation box, and the minimum distance between spheres. The resulting representative cell is a cuboid with a square face of
B
x
×
B
x
and height
B
z
. The geometries were iteratively generated until the target particle size distribution (PSD) was recovered within a fixed error threshold (see Supplementary Note S1 for further details). The resulting cathode microstructures, as those represented in Figs. 1B–C for different values of
B
x
, were modeled as ensembles of spheres, representative of the active material (blue spheres) and the aggregates of the CB conductive additive (black spheres). The latter consists of particles with dimensions in the order of tens or hundreds of nanometers, which form porous aggregates in the order of micrometers during the cell manufacturing process [11,19,20]. In this work, the CB particles added to the cathode are in the order of the micrometers, mimicking the properties of primary CB aggregates rather than those of individual nanoparticles, thus limiting the computational cost of handling too many geometric objects. For the sake of simplicity, aggregates of CB nanoparticles will be referred to as CB particles. The numerical PSDs obtained for different values of
B
x
are shown in Fig. 1D (colored symbols) and compared with the experimental values reported in the NMC data sheet (solid black line), obtaining good agreement for different box sizes. Electrical connection between neighboring NMC particles is ensured by the CB aggregates, which were randomly distributed on the surface of the NMC particles until the target volume fraction is reached. Both the NMC and CB particles distributions were checked to meet their respective volume fractions listed in Table 1 within 1% of error.

The procedure outlined above allows to obtain configurations representative of the original packing density, with realistic CB percolation and following a target experimental PSD. However, the resulting structures are quite complex, with even the smallest configurations generated comprising over 1200 geometric entities, with some particles not contributing to the formation of the percolation network. To simplify these structures, the individual CB particles forming clusters, i.e. ensembles of overlapping CB particles, have been replaced by an equivalent object that mimics the geometry and transport properties of the cluster, significantly reducing the microstructure complexity (compare Figs. 1B and 1E). Here, two different aggregation procedures are considered: all the CB clusters were replaced by either equivalent cylinders or by equivalent spheres (see Fig. 1F); a mixed configuration with both spheres and cylinders was not considered. As an example, Fig. 1F shows a 6-particle CB cluster and its simplification by either a cylinder or a sphere. Considering the cylinder, the first principal axis, highlighted in red, connects the centers of the outermost particles of the aggregate, extends to their surface, and defines the maximum extension of the aggregate
R
1
.
R
2
is defined as the maximum extension of the aggregate perpendicular to the first principal axis (see the green segment connecting the first principal axis to the center of the most perpendicularly distant particle); the green cylinder in Fig. 1F visually shows the extension of
R
2
. Similarly,
R
3
is defined as the maximum extension of the cluster in the third perpendicular direction (see the blue cylinder). Instead, spherical aggregates (shown as a green sphere in Fig. 1F) are generated considering a cluster radius
R
c
=
R
g y r
+
R
¯
, where
R
g y r
represents the cluster gyration radius (further details in Methods) and
R
¯
is the average particle radius within the cluster. The transport properties of both clusters configurations were evaluated via the fractal aggregation theory (see Methods).

A preliminary size independence study (see Methods) was performed to assess the minimum allowable size of the simulation box
B
x
(see Fig. 1C): larger box sizes result in more complex geometries, increase the computational cost, but do not necessarily increase the accuracy of the simulation results. The main results of the structure analysis are shown in Fig. 2. The parameters considered for this analysis are directly related to the cathode performance and, notably, they did not show any significant dependence on the box size, although the data variance tended to decrease for larger box sizes. Instead, the results highlight a marked dependence on the CB volume fraction
ϕ
C B
. The average values (black data) were evaluated over the 30 configurations generated with different
B
x
.

Figs. 2A-B show the percentage of NMC particles not connected to the positive current collector and the percentage of CB not contributing to the conductive network, both of which can measure the efficiency of the materials utilization. Higher NMC isolation from the conductive network results in lower energy densities than their nominal values, as these particles could not participate to intercalation. Effective CB utilization, on the other hand, depends on minimizing the number of CB particles or clusters that contact only a single NMC particle: this reduce the NMC-catholyte interface and increase the ionic tortuosity of the cathode without increasing its average electrical conductivity, resulting in reduced performance. Both parameters showed a declining trend with increasing CB volume fraction
ϕ
C B
. The percentage of isolated NMC particles exhibited an exponential decrease, dropping from 13% to less than 3% when
ϕ
C B
increased from 4.8% to 10% (see, Fig. 2A). Similarly, the non-percolating CB particles decreased linearly with
ϕ
C B
from 60% to 45% (see, Fig. 2B). These results show that the proposed approach allows to connect most NMC particles to the positive current collector within a feasible range of
ϕ
C B
. Instead, large part of the CB might remain unused, highlighting a limit of the cathode microstructure. Higher
ϕ
C B
also led to an increase in the average number of neighbors per NMC particle, namely two adjacent NMC particles connected by CB, from (3.7 ± 1.7) to (6.7 ± 2.4); however, the high variance of the data does not allow to infer a significant change in the percolation state of NMC (see Fig. 2C). Consequently, the average NMC-catholyte active surface tends to decrease linearly with
ϕ
C B
, remaining higher than 80% at the highest CB concentration tested, owing to the hypothesis of non-overlapping particles (see Fig. 2D).

Analysis of CB clusters revealed a weak dependence of cluster shape on CB volume fraction. The average number of CB particles per cluster showed a modest upward trend, ranging from (2.3 ± 0.2) to (3.0 ± 0.6) (see Fig. 2E). However, while average number of CB per cluster remains stable for each
ϕ
C B
investigated, the large variety of cluster sizes within each configuration precludes any significant statistical significance due to the large data variance. The average size and shape of CB clusters was marginally affected by higher values of
ϕ
C B
: considering spherical clusters, their average radius showed no correlation with
ϕ
C B
, maintaining a constant value of
R
c
=
( 1 . 0 ± 0 . 2 )
μm; similarly, for cylindrical clusters, the ratio
R
3
/
R
1
remained stable at about 0.57, while
R
2
/
R
1
showed a slight increase from 0.67 to 0.73, proof of their unaltered shape. As a consequence, the average equivalent electrical conductivity of CB clusters does not change significantly with increasing
ϕ
C B
, remaining stable at approximately (83.7 ± 6.2) S/m (see Fig. 2F), relatively close to the assumed 100 S/m of pure CB particles. Finally, under the current hypothesis, the cluster analysis revealed that an increase in the CB volume fraction results in the formation of a higher number of small clusters rather than extended clusters capable of establishing significant percolation pathways, which could dramatically enhance the cathode electrical conductivity. This implies that although performance is expected to improve with higher
ϕ
C B
(reduced isolated particles, more efficient CB distribution, higher average number of neighbors), the optimal performance has not been attained yet due to the low percolation of CB particles.

To assess the impact of different assumptions on cell performance and to understand which configuration best mimics real cathodes, a comparison was made between the different methods used to represent the CB in terms of cathode tortuosity and effective electrical conductivity
σ
e f f
using FEM simulations (see Methods). The simulation results were compared with experimental values obtained from Refs. [21,22] for similar configurations. For statistical reliability, the results from three distinct configurations with identical microstructure parameters were averaged. Besides the configurations with fully explicit CB particles and the two simplifying shapes assumed for the clusters (namely cylinders or spheres, see Fig. 1F), it was also considered a configuration where the CB was assumed to be homogeneously distributed within the solid catholyte, as commonly adopted in several references.

The results, shown in Fig. 3A, include schematics of the different microstructure assumptions considered. Notably, the configuration with explicit CB particles (blue data) closely matches the experimental results (black lines), effectively mirroring the cathode properties while providing a simple geometric representation. In contrast, the simplistic assumption of homogeneously dispersed CB (green data) overestimates considerably the cathode performance. This effect is likely due to the resulting optimal percolation, where all particles are directly connected to the current collector via a virtually continuous aggregate of CB, yielding a predicted electrical conductivity an order of magnitude higher than that of the explicit configuration and the experimental results. Simplifying the CB network with equivalent spherical or cylindrical clusters lower the simulation complexity, reducing the required mesh elements by about 40% at
ϕ
C B
= 4
.8% and by 70% at 10%. However, it introduces errors at higher
ϕ
C B
: while the three configurations yield similar electrical conductivity at 4.8% and 7.5% of
ϕ
C B
, cylindrical aggregates (magenta data) exceed all other predictions at 10%. This discrepancy is due to the high connectivity offered by the cylinders, which are designed to encompass all particles contained in each cluster: in the case of branched clusters with
R
2
/
R
1
→ 1
, this leads to an overestimation of cluster volume, increasing the average number of NMC neighbors. Similarly, spherical clusters (red data) tend to increase NMC connectivity at high volume fractions; nevertheless, this approach remains statistically consistent with experimental results, making it a preferable choice for simplifying the simulation domain.

These results emphasize the importance of particle percolation over the average electrical conductivity of the additives. This is well illustrated by comparing the performance of the distributed CB configuration with that of the one containing explicit particles: despite a conductivity of (2 ± 1) S/m, much lower than the 100 S/m of pure CB and the approximately 84 S/m of the aggregates, the formation of a single pathway through the simulation domain allows a significant reduction in ohmic losses during operation. Note that the values reported remain one order of magnitude higher than that of bulk NMC811, which is reported to be lower than 0.2 S/m and acts as a bottleneck even compared to the least conductive CB percolation analyzed. Nevertheless, the electrode conductivity of our model closely matches experimental values obtained on similar NMC811 electrodes [2,23,24], suggesting that our approach may yield results more in line with real cells having a similar architecture.

In Fig. 3B, the catholyte tortuosity τ for the different configurations considered was evaluated and compared with experimental data of NMC-based cathodes [23,24] (solid and dashed black lines) and Bruggeman’s theoretical results [25] for dispersed spherical obstacles (solid gray line). Results in Fig. 3B are reported in terms of the SC volume fraction
ϕ
S C
= 1 −
ϕ
N M C
−
ϕ
C B
, representing the total porosity of the electrode allowing for ionic transport. As expected, the homogeneously dispersed CB configuration (green data) resulted in tortuosity values consistent with Bruggeman theory, as the hypotheses of the two models are in agreement. However, comparison with experimental data suggests that this configuration may underestimate catholyte tortuosity, potentially leading to a significant overestimation of battery performance at high currents. Instead, tortuosity values for configurations with explicit CB (blue data) and spherical clusters (red data) are in good agreement with experimental results. Finally, cylindrical clusters (magenta data) resulted in much higher tortuosity at high values of
ϕ
C B
due to the excessive volume occupied by the aggregates: as observed for
σ
e f f
, τ also showed high variability across the three simulated setups. These results suggest that explicit CB and spherical aggregates can reasonably recover the transport properties of the catholyte and can be safely used to derive reliable electrochemical predictions.

In summary, the proposed model effectively captures the essential transport properties of electrodes composed of spherical particles, such as those comprising NMC materials. These analyses indicate that explicit inclusion of conductive carbon black aggregates yields the most accurate results. Alternatively, employing spherical clusters simplifies the simulation domain while maintaining representative transfer properties. Instead, the cylindrical clustering approach fails to provide reliable transfer properties at high CB volume fractions and will not be utilized in subsequent simulations.

Electrochemical simulations were performed to evaluate the effect of the different assumed cathode geometries: the configurations with homogenized, explicit and spherical clustered CB are referred to as Conf. 1, Conf. 2 and Conf. 3, respectively. To ensure a fair comparison, the simulations were performed considering the same arrangement of NMC particles for all simulated configurations, while the CB distribution was varied to adapt to the different volume fractions considered. Each configuration was tested at a constant current of C/10, to achieve a deep charge/discharge due to lower polarization losses; several charge and discharge cycles were performed until steady state conditions were evaluated. To limit the computational effort, considering Conf. 2 and Conf. 3, the CB particles that do not contribute to the cathode connectivity, i.e. those that are simply deposited on the NMC surface and reduce the exchange area with the catholyte, were removed from the configuration. However, to account for their effect, the multiplying coefficient
( 1 − χ )
was introduced in the Butler–Volmer equation to hinder Li intercalation (see Methods), where χ is the fraction of the CB-NMC contact area that does not contribute to the electrical connectivity. In particular, the coefficient χ was evaluated as 4.9%, 6.6% and 9.7% for the three considered increasing values of
ϕ
C B
, respectively, via the geometry generation algorithm. This procedure allowed to limit the number of CB particles or aggregates included in the domain by approximately 74%, drastically reducing the computational cost. The coordinates data required to replicate the setups simulated in this Section are available as Supplementary Materials.

The results are shown in Fig. 4. Considering
ϕ
C B
= 4 . 8 %
, the cell potentials for the different cathode configurations assumed are compared as a function of state of charge in Fig. 4A. Wider curves correspond to more efficient use of the battery, with greater depth of charge and discharge. It can be observed that changing the representation model of the CB has a major impact on the cell performance. Conf. 2 leads to large losses during operation, resulting in low cell utilization and an available state of charge window in the range of 0.60 – 0.75. Moving to Conf. 3 and Conf. 1, on the other hand, the available state of charge increases to 0.50 – 0.85 and 0.40 – 0.99, respectively, despite presenting the same NMC arrangement. Clearly, the observed difference in performance is due to the connectivity between the active material and the positive current collector provided by the different CB models. This is also reflected in a strong difference in the longitudinal distribution of lithium within the cathode. For example, Fig. 4B shows the relative lithium concentration
c /
c
m a x
for
ϕ
C B
= 4 . 8 %
at the end of the charge for the different cathode structure models studied. As can be seen, in Conf. 1 the concentration is homogeneous, while considering Conf. 2 and Conf. 3, the concentration profile is markedly non-homogeneous, with lower relative concentrations near the current collector (marked in the figure as CC+). Note also that the explicit representation of the CB, either by Conf. 2 or Conf. 3, allows to capture the disconnection between NMC particles, shown in red in Fig. 4B: considering Conf. 1, all the active material particles are connected, while in Conf. 3 and Conf. 2 their number increases progressively. This undoubtedly contributes to the difference in charge profiles observed in Fig. 4A: a larger volume of active material not connected to the CB network contributes to a reduced charge/discharge depth, as it might happen in real cells. The interconnection caused by the explicit CB model also leads to a marked gradient in the lithium concentration perpendicular to the current collector, as can be seen in Fig. 4B. This behavior is due to the drop of the electric potential in the particles further away from the current collector or, in general, in those connected to the current collector with a high resistance path. As a result, particles closer to or in direct contact with the current collector de-intercalate lithium faster. Increasing the connectivity (e.g., higher
ϕ
C B
) mitigates this behavior: at the limit, ideal percolation, as in Conf. 1 allows the NMC particles to behave almost independently of their distance from the current collector, resulting in a homogeneous lithium distribution. A more detailed discussion can be found in Supplementary Note S2.

As expected, increasing the volume fraction of carbon black increases the charge and discharge depth (see Fig. 4C): for example, considering Conf. 2, the cell state of charge increases from 0.60 – 0.75 at
ϕ
C B
= 4 . 8 %
(solid blue line, Fig. 4A) to approximately 0.45 – 0.9 at
ϕ
C B
= 7 . 5 %
and
ϕ
C B
= 10 %
(see the solid red and black lines in Fig. 4C). Even at higher volume fractions, considering the CB clusters (i.e., Conf. 3) leads to an enhanced cell performance (see dashed lines): in addition to reducing the fraction of isolated active material, it leads to an increased connectivity of the cathode mainly due to the larger size of the CB spheres. This is confirmed by the comparison of the percentage of NMC particles connected via the CB network and the average number of neighbors for Conf. 2 and Conf. 3 as a function of
ϕ
C B
, reported in Fig. 4D–E. The results show that the average percentage of isolated particles decreases slightly for all the tested
ϕ
C B
; instead, the average number of neighbors increases markedly for Conf. 3 with respect to Conf. 2. This is consistent with the higher electrical conductivity of the cathode observed in Fig. 3A and the smoother concentration profiles along the cathode length observed in Fig. 4B. Interestingly, when considering Conf. 1 (dotted curves in Figs. 4A and 4C), no significant differences in cell behavior are observed as the concentration varies. This behavior is not reflected by the experimental evidence and shows the limitations of such a simplified model, which overestimates the connectivity of the considered NMC particles and limits the benefits of developing 3D microscale-resolved cell models.

The difference in the electrochemical behavior between the microstructure models could be further examined by considering higher, more demanding current densities, leading the cell to work in limiting conditions. For this reason, the configuration at
ϕ
C B
= 10 %
was also simulated with increasing C-rates until charge/discharge was inhibited by excessive losses. The comparison was limited to a single case; the performance of Conf. 2 (i.e., the explicit CB model) was compared with that of Conf. 1 (i.e., the homogenized model), while spherical clusters were excluded from the comparison as they have already been shown to cause an overestimation of cell performance. The results shown in Fig. 5 underline the major impact of considering an explicit percolation network compared to its homogenized counterpart. As the applied current density increases from C/10 to C/2 (see Fig. 5A–C), the cell performance by the explicit model (blue curves), decreases sharply. However, the energy transferred during discharge remains consistent with the magnitude observed for Conf. 1 (red curves), approximately in the range of hundreds of μ Ah/cm2. In contrast, when the applied current is further increased to 1C and 2C, the explicit model shows that excessive losses make the battery unable to efficiently store and release energy: in these cases, the energy discharged is on the order of a few nAh/cm2 (see Fig. 5D). Conversely, considering Conf. 1, the cell maintains good performance, with transferred energies almost five orders of magnitude higher than those obtained with Conf. 2 (see Fig. 5E). Notably, the coulombic cell efficiency shows a significant difference between the two models: while Conf.1 consistently maintains a coulombic efficiency above 99%, the explicit CB model shows a progressive decrease from
> 98 %
at C/10 and C/5, to 36% at C/2, and less than 5% at higher currents. This trend is consistent with experimental evidences suggesting that solid-state cells have difficulty maintaining high specific capacity at high current densities [26].

Finally, the results presented in Sections 3–4 suggest that performance variability should be expected between different configurations created with the same target parameters due to the connectivity provided by the random placement of CB and AM particles. This aspect was investigated repeating the electrochemical simulations with (i) two additional configurations with
B
x
= 20
μm and (ii) a configuration with
B
x
= 30
μm, both at
ϕ
C B
= 4 . 8 %
. The simulations were performed considering the explicit representation of CB particles (namely, Conf. 2), and a constant applied current of C/10. The SOC and the energy transferred during charging and discharging for the different configurations tested are compared in Fig. 6A-B. As expected, their average performance differs depending on the connectivity of the particles: the SOC achieved during charging is about 60% for all the configurations considered and increases between 75% and 80% at the end of the discharge, which translates into a variability of approximately of 3%. Similarly, the energy transferred during charging and discharging is (363 ± 39) μ Ah/cm2 and (358 ± 37) μ Ah/cm2 respectively, while the coulombic efficiency remains
> 97 %
for all configurations. Fig. 6C shows the normalized lithium concentration of the active material at the end of charging for all 4 configurations studied. The observed inhomogeneities are due to the randomized distribution of the CB particles: for example, the areas close to the current collector with poor connection to the rest of the cathode material experience faster delithiation, thus reaching the cutoff potential earlier. These areas are distributed similarly in all configurations; furthermore, also the lithium concentration profile along the cathode thickness shows similar trends. This analysis, as expected, highlights the need to perform several simulations when evaluating the performance of a given cell architecture, since the randomness of the generation protocol produces a variability of about 10% in the energy stored and released by the cell. The difference observed between the configuration obtained with
B
x
= 30
μm and
B
x
= 20
μm is limited and can be justified in the light of the variability observed between the configurations tested with
B
x
= 30
μm. These simulations show that the effect of microstructure variability on electrochemical performance is of the same order of magnitude as that potentially due to box size, thus supporting our hypothesis of box size independence already investigated in Section 3.

This work presents a novel and simplified approach to generate 3D-resolved microstructures of composite electrodes based on quasi-spherical particles, such as secondary NMC. This approach was applied to an argyrodite-based solid state cell enriched with carbon black (CB), here explicitly modeled as particles in the order of 10−1 μm –
1
0
0
μm. The method, implemented in Octave using a fall-and-roll approach, accurately captures the material packing characteristics and the target volume fractions of the materials considered.

The microstructural properties were tested for box-size independence; key aspects such as active material connectivity, intercalation surface area, and particle isolation were investigated across different CB volume fractions. Statistical accuracy was ensured by generating multiple configurations for each one tested, yielding consistent results that reflect real-world scenarios. The effectiveness of the proposed approach was tested by comparing the cathode electrical conductivity and catholyte tortuosity with reference experimental and analytical data. The results obtained show that the electrical conductivity is well recovered by the proposed model. Instead, considering the carbon black as homogenized within the solid catholyte leads to a deviation from experiments by one order of magnitude. Finally, representing the interconnected CB particles as homogenized clusters, either spherical or cylindrical, provided reliable results for low volume fractions of carbon black.

Electrochemical simulations at constant currents were performed using the FEM software COMSOL Multiphysics®, which highlighted the major impact of the conductive additive representation. The cell performance with homogenized CB was insensitive to the volume fraction considered, showing up to +400% energy storage capacity with respect to considering explicit CB particles. Instead, considering homogenized CB clusters led to a partial overestimation of cell performance due to higher connectivity. However, the differences between the setups considered were mitigated at higher CB volume fractions. The major impact of modeling an explicit percolation network over its homogenized counterpart was underscored by performing simulations at higher currents. Transitioning from C/10 to 2C, the explicit CB model showed a sharp decrease in both cell performance and its coulombic efficiency with increasing current density, consistent with experimental findings; instead, the homogenized model maintained the same magnitude of energy transferred during charge and discharge, and a coulombic efficiency always higher than 99%. Finally, the variability of the electrochemical performance due to the randomness of the microstructure generation was evaluated by repeating electrochemical simulations at C/10 on four different microstructures generated also considering increasing box size. The results allowed to estimate an uncertainty of about 10% on the energy transferred during charging and discharging.

This work marks an efficient alternative with respect to existing references of 3D-resolved model, offering an accurate representation of complex behaviors at a reduced computational cost. The results presented show the limitations of homogenization approaches, which tend to overestimate the effects of CB percolations and, thus, the cell performance. Furthermore, the simplicity and flexibility of our approach allows for easy generation of diverse microstructures without complicated characterization techniques, facilitating the exploration of novel cell designs. Future improvements include extensive validation against experimental and simulation techniques such as DEM simulations. In addition, the representation of a polymeric binder would increase its applicability to other solid-state architectures or traditional liquid-based electrolytes, promoting the advancement of next-generation cell architectures.

Considering the box size independence analysis,
B
x
was varied between 20 μm and 30 μm with a step size of 5 μm, while the cathode thickness
B
z
was considered fixed at 31.3 μm, imposed by the cell manufacturing parameters (see Table 1). Box sizes lower than 20 μm would not allow to recover the desired particle size distribution. The box-size independence analysis was performed for all the considered values of CB volume fraction, namely 4.8%, 7.5% and 10%. To maintain statistical representativeness, ten configurations were generated for each box size, each respecting the experimental particle size distribution, and the results were averaged. Note that, on average, the number of NMC particles considered increased from approximately 180 to 380 for the smallest and largest box sizes, respectively. Similarly, the number of CB particles increased on average from 1000 to 2300.

A custom clustering algorithm was implemented to sort the CB particles into different clusters: the i-th particle is assigned to the j-th cluster if it overlaps with any particle belonging to that cluster; the lists of particles generated by the algorithm were used to identify their position, shape, and equivalent properties. The electrical conductivity of the CB clusters was evaluated taking into account the effect of percolation by the fractal aggregation theory [27–30], which is crucial to obtain a representative behavior of the considered microstructure. Further details are reported in Supplementary Note S3.

To catholyte tortuosity τ and cathode electrical conductivity
σ
e f f
were evaluated via FEM simulations in COMSOL Multiphysics® v6.2. The Laplace equation was solved in either the catholyte or the electron conducting regions to evaluate each property. Further details on the mathematical model and its implementation are reported in Supplementary Note S4.

The electrochemical model, implemented in the FEM software COMSOL Multiphysics®, solves the charge conservation equations in the SC, NMC and CB domains and the mass conservation of Li in the NMC domains. The SC was modeled as a single-ion conductor, while intercalation of lithium in the NMC was modeled according to the Butler–Volmer equation. Further details on the electrochemical model are reported in Supplementary Notes S5.
All the physicochemical parameters required by the simulations are reported in Supplementary Table S1.

The combined uncertainties of experimental measurements were estimated as [31]:
U = k
u
A
2
+
u
B
2
, where
u
A
are the uncertainty components estimated from the statistical analysis of the measured values,
u
B
are the uncertainty components estimated from a-priori information,
k = 2
is the coverage factor. The uncertainty components
u
A
were estimated as
u
A
= 0 . 5
|
x
m a x
−
x
m i n
|
, where x is the variable considered. All the error bars reported in the figures show a
± U
interval.

ce:list-item.ce:para: FIB-SEM: Focused Ion Beam Scanning Electron Microscopy

ce:section.ce:para: Matteo Alberghini: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Giulia Blanco: Visualization, Validation, Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Andrea Bertinetti: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Alessio Tommasi: Writing – original draft, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Mauro Sgroi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was performed as part of the SOLiDIFY project, funded by the European Union’s Horizon 2020 research and innovation program under grant agreement No. 875557, and the HEDAsupercap project, funded by the European Union’s Horizon Europe research and innovation action under grant agreement No. 101092189.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary material related to this article.
MMC S1
Supplementary notes with details on the implementation of the numerical model.
MMC S2
MMC S3
